coredata.dc:title: Solvent-engineered performance improvement of graphene quantum dot sensitized solar cells with nitrogen functionalized GQD photosensitizers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Dey, Tamal
Ghorai, Arup
Das, Soumen
Ray, Samit K.
coredata.dc:description: We report for the first time that the choice of solvent used in the synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) has a significant effect on the photovoltaic performance of quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSSC) where N-GQDs work as photosensitizers. Switching from protic water (H2O) solvent to aprotic N-N dimethylformamide (DMF) causes significant change in structural, photophysical and electronic properties of N-GQDs working as active materials in QDSSC. Unlike traditional dye-sensitized solar cells, N-GQDs replace toxic and readily degradable organic dyes as an active material, due to enhanced stability, cost-efficient fabrication, and environment-friendly processing. We discussed compositional, optical, and electronic properties of N-GQDs synthesized in different solvents, and their role in the photoconversion efficiency of quantum dots sensitized solar cells. Spectroscopic results reveal that nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with green emission (G-GQDs) have relatively shorter effective conjugation length and lower content of pyrrolic nitrogen groups because water is a polar protic solvent, which suppresses the water elimination reaction by donating protons to the reaction medium. On the contrary, DMF, a polar aprotic solvent, cannot donate hydrogen, leading to enhanced nitrogen units in the graphene quantum dots framework and red emission (R-GQDs), thus improving carrier density and transport properties. QDSSCs fabricated from R-GQDs exhibit photovoltaic performance (PCE > 5.0%) superior to other previously reported GQD sensitized solar cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dot sensitized solar cells
Nitrogen functionalized GQDs
Graphene quantum dots
QDSSC
Photovoltaic device
Photoconversion efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mesoporous TiO2 film formation on FTO-coated glass substrates
Adsorption of nitrogen doped GQDs on mesoporous TiO2

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of synthesis procedure of nitrogen doped GQDs and digital photographs (a) G-GQD formation in H2O and (b) R-GQD formation in DMF.
Transmission electron micrographs of (a) G-GQDs and (b) R-GQDs. High-resolution TEM shows the interplanar spacing in (c) G-GQDs and (d) R-GQDs nanocrystals, respectively.
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy showing the binding energy of C 1s and N 1s electrons. C 1s spectra of (a) G-GQDs and (b) R-GQDs. N 1s spectra of (c) G-GQDs and (d) R-GQDs.

Optical characteristics of the synthesized GQD samples. (a) Absorption spectra of N-doped and undoped GQDs, (b) Comparative PL spectra of G-GQDs and R-GQDs. Deconvoluted PL spectra of (c) G-GQDs and (d) R-GQDs.

(a) Schematic diagram of the QDSSCs using N-doped GQDs. (b) Energy band diagram of the G- and R-GQDs sensitized solar cell devices. (c) Photovoltaic characteristics of the devices using G- and R-GQDs showing the short circuit current density and open circuit voltage. (d) External quantum efficiency (EQE) of N-doped GQDs sensitized solar cells.

Impedance spectroscopy (Nyquist plots) of the fabricated N-doped GQD sensitized solar cells. (a) The equivalent circuit to fit the experimental data. Nyquist plots under dark and illuminated conditions for (b) G-GQDs and (c) R-GQDs.
Comparative performances of G-GQDs and R-GQDs.
Fitted parameters from the impedance spectroscopy).
Comparison of performance of reported GQDs sensitized photovoltaic devices.

head.ce:title: Solvent-engineered performance improvement of graphene quantum dot sensitized solar cells with nitrogen functionalized GQD photosensitizers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nanoscience and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, 721302, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Nanoscience and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, 721302, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, 721302, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, 721302, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photo Conversion Efficiency of Solvent Engineered and Nitrogen Functionalized Graphene Quantum Dots Sensitized Solar Cells.

We discussed compositional, optical and electronic properties of N-GQDs synthesized in different solvents, and their role in the photoconversion efficiency of quantum dots sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs). Solar cells fabricated from R-GQDs synthesized in DMF solvent show much superior performance (∼5.3%) compared to the solar cells fabricated from G-GQDs (∼0.7%) synthesized in water solvent, which is attributed to higher Jsc, Voc and fill factor.

We report for the first time that the choice of solvent used in the synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) has a significant effect on the photovoltaic performance of quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSSC) where N-GQDs work as photosensitizers. Switching from protic water (H2O) solvent to aprotic N-N dimethylformamide (DMF) causes significant change in structural, photophysical and electronic properties of N-GQDs working as active materials in QDSSC. Unlike traditional dye-sensitized solar cells, N-GQDs replace toxic and readily degradable organic dyes as an active material, due to enhanced stability, cost-efficient fabrication, and environment-friendly processing. We discussed compositional, optical, and electronic properties of N-GQDs synthesized in different solvents, and their role in the photoconversion efficiency of quantum dots sensitized solar cells. Spectroscopic results reveal that nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with green emission (G-GQDs) have relatively shorter effective conjugation length and lower content of pyrrolic nitrogen groups because water is a polar protic solvent, which suppresses the water elimination reaction by donating protons to the reaction medium. On the contrary, DMF, a polar aprotic solvent, cannot donate hydrogen, leading to enhanced nitrogen units in the graphene quantum dots framework and red emission (R-GQDs), thus improving carrier density and transport properties. QDSSCs fabricated from R-GQDs exhibit photovoltaic performance (PCE > 5.0%) superior to other previously reported GQD sensitized solar cells.

ce:section.ce:para: For the last few decades, photovoltaics technology recorded an annual growth rate of > 20%, route (Wu et al., 2015). Silicon based first generation and thin film based second generation solar cells have already proved their ability for high conversion efficiency devices essential to meet the rapidly growing global renewable energy demand. However, their high production cost and environmental impact limit their widespread terrestrial applications, which stimulated the search for new earth abundant, environmental friendly and low-cost photovoltaic materials. Therefore, third-generation photovoltaic devices, including dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) (Grätzel, 2003, 2001), organic/polymer solar cells (OSCs) (Lee et al., 2020), perovskite solar cells (PSCs) (Kim et al., 2020), quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs) (Mora-Seró, 2020; Sahu et al., 2020) etc. have attracted tremendous attention. DSSCs reduce the manufacturing cost by 10 to 20% (“Cost reduction in the solar industry - Materials Today,” n.d.), as compared to silicon solar cells production cost, but the organic dyes are readily degraded under aging and light exposure, restricting their long term use for terrestrial applications. For example, the most popular organic dyes, ruthenium dyes (Tomar et al., 2020) (C26H16N6O8RuS2) possess heavy metal toxicity, and is quite rare. On the other hand, zinc porphyrin (YD2-o-C8) dyes (Li et al., 2021) have problems of low synthetic yield and high toxicity. Solution processed quantum dots sensitized solar cells possess the advantages of high environmental stability and low-cost fabrication methods due to a simple device structure derived from their analogous DSSC counterpart. Generally, quantum dots of traditional semiconductors like CdS (Prima et al., 2021), CdSe (Jin et al., 2021), PbS (Jin et al., 2021), CZTS (Singh et al., 2021) and even carbons are used either as a counter electrode (CE) (Zhang et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2017) or co-sensitizer (AlGhamdi et al., 2020; Song et al., 2021) for the fabrication of QDSSCs, but the toxicity of transition metal sulphide quantum dots is a major concern. To circumvent the above bottlenecks, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have recently attracted immense limelight [7,8] as viable replacement of sensitizers and high-cost counter electrode like Pt. Similar to graphene, GQDs also possess good crystallinity as well as high conductivity due to the presence of conducting sp2 framework (Mahalingam et al., 2021). The presence of strong quantum confinement effect, edge effect, strong visible light absorption and emission, and most importantly high surface-to-volume ratio make GQDs very attractive for future optoelectronic devices such as solar cells (Gupta et al., 2011; Lee et al., 2015; Sharma and Jha, 2019), photo detectors (Dey et al., 2020; Saeidi et al., 2020), LEDs (Ezhov et al., 2020; Tian et al., 2018); and energy storage devices like supercapacitors (Ashourdan et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2014) and fuel cells (Shaari et al., 2021). GQDs have recently been employed as dopants in a counter electrode of DSSC; like GQD decorated carbon aerogel (Wang and Lu, 2015) or GQD doped polypyrrole (Chen et al., 2013a), to enhance the catalytic activity of the counter electrode. Tunable band gap by the control of size or composition, high molar extinction coefficient of ∼ 106 M−1 cm−1 (Wang et al., 2014), and negligible optical, thermal, and moisture related degradations in comparison to dye molecules or even lead halide perovskites (Mahato et al., 2020; Pal et al., 2021) are characteristic features of GQDs as a light-harvesting material, making them very promising candidates for ambient stable photovoltaic devices. Additionally, GQDs have a number of extraordinary properties such as the possibility of multiple exciton generation (MEG) (Beard, 2011), which could help quantum dot based solar cells to surpass the Shockley–Queisser photoconversion efficiency limit (Ehrler et al., 2020). However, there are only a few reports of the utilization of GQDs as a high conducting as well as visible light absorbing material for DSSCs. Liu et al. (Liu et al., 2017) recently reported GQDs decorated strontium ruthenate (SrRuO3) as counter electrode for high efficiency dye sensitized solar cell with an efficiency of 7.16%. Here GQDs provide an effective electron transportation pathway by providing a conducting sp2 network leading to longer mean free path of electrons. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess all the qualities of a good sensitizer i.e., a conduction band edge higher than that of an electron transporting material for effective electron injection and excellent stability towards light, heat, and electrolyte.

For further improvements on solar cell efficacy, the introduction of atomic impurities e.g. nitrogen, boron, oxygen, phosphorous, and sulfur into the carbon sp2 lattice can cause disorientation of homogeneously conjugated electron cloud, which may be useful to reduce the total internal resistance of counter electrodes (Xie et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2011). Nitrogen sits next to carbon in the periodic table with closer atomic radius (0.77 Å and 0.70 Å, respectively) but has a higher electronegativity (3.04) than carbon (2.55), making it easier to incorporate nitrogen into carbon framework (Gao et al., 2013), which regulates the surface properties by modifying the charge of doped domains (Li et al., 2017). Moreover, N atoms provide the graphitic network with additional π-electrons (Li et al., 2012).

In this work, we have synthesized nitrogen functionalized graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) by a cost effective and simple hydrothermal approach. Interestingly, we have observed that with the use of different solvents i.e. protic (H2O) or aprotic polar N-N dimethylformamide (DMF) solvents the properties of the as-synthesized N-GQDs are drastically changed. With the use of aprotic polar solvent DMF in the hydrothermal reaction, GQDs show red emission characteristics (R-GQDs) while they emit green color (G-GQDs) for the polar protic solvent H2O. The polar aprotic solvent has been found to promote the formation of extended conjugation, leading to a higher absorption coefficient as well as higher charge carrier mobility. Finally, we report the photovoltaic characteristics of both nitrogen-functionalized graphene quantum dots (R- & G-GQDs) as sensitizing materials in TiO2 based quantum dots sensitized solar cells for the first time. The higher absorptive and conductive nature of R-GQDs are reflected in the device performance with the device showing an efficiency of 5.3%, an increase by ∼ 700% over the G-GQDs, which is attributed to the efficient charge transport and superior absorption characteristics.
All the reagents were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (purity of all the chemicals are > 99.0%).

Two different types (green and red) of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots were prepared by solvothermal reactions of Citric acid and Urea in two different solvents. We have labelled them as G-GQDs and R-GQDs for green and red emitting quantum dots, respectively.
(a)
G-GQD Preparation: 210 mg (1.0 mmol) of citric acid monohydrate (CA; C6H8O7·H2O) and 180 mg (3.0 mmol) of urea were dissolved in 5.0 mL of deionized (DI) water. The resultant solution (pH ∼ 6) was put into an autoclave chamber and was heated in a furnace at 200 °C for 8 h. The obtained product was filtrated and 2.0 mL of ethanol was added. Ethanol helps in reduction of surface tension which prevents agglomeration of the quantum dots. Thus the green-colored solution of G-GQDs in water was obtained after purification through centrifugation.
(b)
R-GQD Preparation: 210 mg (1.0 mmol) of citric acid monohydrate (CA; C6H8O7·H2O) and 180 mg (3.0 mmol) of urea were dissolved 5.0 mL of N, N-Dimethyl-Formamide (DMF). The resultant solution (pH ∼ 7) was put into an autoclave chamber and was heated in a furnace at 200 °C for 8 h. The obtained product was filtrated and 2.0 mL of ethanol was added. A red-colored solution of R-GQDs in DMF was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mesoporous TiO2 film formation on FTO-coated glass substrates
ce:section.ce:para: Pre-cleaned fluorinated tin oxide (FTO) coated glass substrates, which act as transparent electrodes, were uniformly coated with commercially procured TiO2 paste (sigma Aldrich) by doctor-bladed method. These coated substrates were heated to 450 °C by ramp-heating technique to transform the TiO2 film into mesoporous surface for enhanced adsorption of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of nitrogen doped GQDs on mesoporous TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: FTO substrates with mesoporous TiO2 layer were dip-coated in nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots solution so that they are adsorbed on the surface for 24 hrs. Then, they were washed with ethanol to remove un-adsorbed N-GQDs followed by dried in ambient condition for further use.

A solution of H2PtCl6 (2 wt%) was spin-coated on another clean FTO coated glass substrate and dried at 90 °C for 2 h. After that the substrate was annealed at 450 °C. This resulted in the nucleation of Pt-nanoparticles on the surface. Pt acts as a catalyst for regeneration of the electrolytes, and its catalytic activity is enhanced in nanoparticle form.

0.1(M) LiI, 0.05(M) I2, 0.5(M) t-butyl pyridine and 0.6(M) DMP-II (1, 2-dimethyl-3-propylimidazolium iodide) were dissolved in 10 mL acetonitrile solvent and was stirred for 5–6 h at room temperature under dark condition. This helped the formation of I3 -/I- type redox couple for further device measurement.

TiO2 coated FTO glass substrates and the Pt-nanoparticle coated counter electrodes were sandwiched by paper clips and the electrolyte was dropped along the junction by a pipette during electrical measurements. Finally, the full device was sealed along the edge for gaining stability and to prevent electrolyte evaporation.

Structural characterization of nitrogen functionalized GQDs were performed with a high-resolution TEM (JEOL, model JEM-2100F) with a CCD detector. Optical absorption spectra (UV–visual) were acquired using a Avaspec-3648 spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired by a TRIAX-320 monochromator and Hamamatsu R928 PMT detector; under excitation of using a He − Cd laser of excitation wavelength ∼325 nm. Electrical measurements of all the devices were performed with a Keithley semiconductor parameter analyzer (4200A-SCS). Impedance spectroscopy was carried out by Gamry Instruments in a two electrode measurement configuration within the frequency range of 10 to 105 Hz

To synthesize N-doped GQDs (Dey et al., 2020) with high fluorescence, citric acid (CA) has been chosen as the carbon source and urea for the N source. When the solvothermal reaction is carried out in H2O, as-synthesized N-doped GQDs show the green emission (hence denoted as G-GQDs). If the reaction is performed in DMF instead, N-doped GQDs exhibit a strong red light emission (hence denoted as R-GQDs), as shown in the schematic diagrams and digital photographs in Fig. 1 . The difference in reaction mechanism is solely dependent on the solvent, which are H2O and DMF. G-GQDs are formed in a polar protic solvent H2O as shown in Fig. 1a. On the other hand, DMF, a polar aprotic solvent, yields R-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 1b. GQDs are formed by the water elimination reaction between inter and intra-molecular hydroxyl groups (–OH) and Hydrogen (-H) to form benzene like rings consisting of 6 carbon atoms, as reported earlier(Dey et al., 2018).

In case of G-GQDs formed in H2O solvent, a continuous donation of protons by the polar protic solvent (water) suppresses the elimination of H2O in CA, leading to the intra molecular reaction more favorable over inter molecular one. This results in shortening of conjugation length and presence of more –COOH groups on the surface and edges of GQDs, and incomplete carbonization, which is also evident in the percentage of functional groups calculated from X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis to be discussed later. Again, acidic environment of citric acid in H2O leads to less effective attachment of nitrogen into the ring through the protonation of amide groups of urea, which is evident in XPS spectra of N1s, i.e. more amount of graphitic N and lesser amount of pyrrolic nitrogen present in the sample.

On the other hand, for R-GQDs synthesis, the polar aprotic solvent DMF cannot freely give away its hydrogen in the reaction medium. So, it promotes the elimination of water molecule from the CA moiety under high temperature and high pressure in the autoclave resulting in prominent intermolecular reaction in CA over intra molecular one. Inter molecular reaction between two CA molecules leads to the complete carbonization process leaving more number of carbon atoms saturated. The amide group of urea is quite free to attach with the sp3 carbon moiety, rather than the core sp2 carbon, under solvothermal condition. This leads to a relatively larger effective conjugation length of GQDs (Qu et al., 2015), abundance of pyrrolic N in the carbon moiety and almost negligible amount of graphitic N, as observed through XPS results to be discussed hereafter. This results in a slightly larger average size of R-GQDs as compared to G-GQDs.

Transmission electron micrographs of G-GQDs and R-GQDs are shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b), respectively, with their corresponding size distribution presented in respective insets. Average lateral size of G-GQDs and R-GQDs is found to be ∼ 1.0 nm and ∼ 3.0 nm, respectively, which are in agreement with our premise of higher conjugation length in R-GQDs. Furthermore, high-resolution TEM images in Fig. 2(c) and (d) present interplanar spacing of G-GQDs and R-GQDs respectively as 0.28 nm and 0.34 nm, respectively, for (1120) set of planes in the hcp unit cell of graphene quantum dots (Dey et al., 2018). The difference in values in the interplanar spacing arises due to more number of –COOH bonds in graphene nano-sheets containing R-GQDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements have been carried out to investigate the chemical, environmental and composition differences in G-GQDs and R-GQDs. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. 3 (a) and (b), can be splitted into three Gaussian peaks for both types of GQDs. The peak with the center at 284.6 eV corresponds to graphitic sp2 carbon (CC/CC). The peaks with centers at 285.8 and 287.6 eV represent sp3 carbon groups (CC, CO, CN), and carbonyl bond (CO), respectively. The peak centered at 288.5 eV corresponds to the carboxylate C(O)O groups. These results exhibit an increased presence of the sp2 carbon content in R-GQDs, indicating towards the increased effective conjugation length of R-GQDs compared to G-GQDs, due to the solvent effect as discussed before.

On the other hand, high-resolution N1s XPS spectra of G-GQDs and R-GQDs (Fig. 3c and d respectively) can be deconvoluted into two Gaussian peaks at, 399.7 eV and 401.1 eV, which are assigned to the pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N, respectively (Jo et al., 2021). The relative fractions of pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N increase and decrease, respectively from G-GQDs to R-GQDs samples. This indicates that the formation of pyrrolic rings is more favorable in an aprotic solvent, as discussed earlier. This is in agreement with previous reports showing the formation of pyrrolic ring via de-hydrolysis between neighboring carboxyl and amide groups of CA and urea respectively (Qu et al., 2014). Pyrrolic nitrogen is mainly responsible for higher charge transportation in nitrogen doped GQD systems (Tian et al., 2020).

Absorption spectra of nitrogen doped G-GQDs and R-GQDs have been compared with that of undoped GQDs (black) in Fig. 4 a. Comparative spectra of N-doped and un-doped GQDs in Fig. 4a clearly suggest superior absorption, suitable for optoelectronic device applications. Both of the absorption spectra of N-doped GQDs exhibit two signature peaks of GQDs at ∼ 270 nm and ∼ 320 nm attributed to π-π* transition of CC bonds in graphitic C-domain and n-π* transitions of CO bonds, respectively. In addition, due to N-doping, we observe an additional broad peak at ∼ 630 nm, due to the C-N conjugations of the pyrrolic bonds (López-Mayorga et al., 2018). It is clear from the spectra that the absorption coefficient of R-GQDs is higher compared to G-GQDs, which is ascribed to higher CC conjugation length, and N concentration, as already supported by the elemental analysis from XPS. PL emission features of both the samples are compared in Fig. 4(b) and the red-shifted emission of R-GQDs is attributed to longer effective conjugation length. A higher intensity of steady state PL also suggests higher recombination lifetime of photo-generated charge carriers in R-GQDs (Lakowicz, 2006).

We have deconvoluted the PL spectra of N-doped GQDs. Upon deconvolution, both the PL spectra exhibit two peaks as shown in Fig. 4(c) and (d). The peak-1 is at the same wavelength (450 nm) for both the samples, which originates from the excitonic quantum confinement effect at the graphitic cores of both G-GQDs and R-GQDs. The other peak (peak-2), however, has a different origin. As we have already discussed in the manuscript, it originates from the n-π* transitions of nitrogen and oxygen functional groups as well as edge states present at the surface. To be precise, the pyrrolic-N groups are the source of the second PL peak. In G-GQDs, the pyrrolic-N group concentration is much lower than the graphitic-N groups, whereas in R-GQDs the pyrrolic-N group dominates over the graphitic-N group. Hence, the peak-2 is more intense for R-GQDs. Also, the effective conjugation length due to these functional groups is larger in case of R-GQDs, hence the peak-2 is slightly red-shifted (∼540 nm) than the peak-2 in G-GQDs (∼520 nm).This is in well agreement with the previous report(Chen et al., 2013b) of red-shifting of the PL peak with the introduction of pyrrole ring into GQDs. In addition, the lesser intra-molecular dehydration reaction in R-GQDs increases the conjugation length in the sample.

From the discussion of synthesis mechanism, optical absorption/emission spectra and elemental analysis, it is evident that R-GQDs have higher absorption coefficient, and efficient charge carrier generation and separation (Lakowicz, 2006). A larger conjugation length of sp2 carbon also indicates towards higher charge carrier density in R-GQDS. On the basis of above observations, we have fabricated N-doped GQD sensitized solar cell devices and measured their photo-conversion efficiency.

Both G-GQDs and R-GQDs have been utilized for the fabrication of quantum dots sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs). A schematic diagram of the fabricated device is shown in Fig. 5 (a). Here we have chosen pre-cleaned fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) having a lower sheet resistance and high temperature sustainability than that of indium doped tin oxide (ITO) as the transparent conducting electrode. A mesoporous TiO2 layer deposited on the substrate serves as the sensitizer medium because of its high surface area and capability of electron transfer to the conducting substrate. We have chosen I3/I- the as a redox potential electrolyte, G-GQDs and R-GQDs as sensitizing materials and Pt as a counter electrode. The final device configuration is given as: FTO/m-TiO2/ (R/G)-GQDs/ Pt/ FTO multilayer structure. Here, instead of a traditional dye in DSSCs, we have used nitrogen doped GQDs (green and red) adsorbed on the surface of the mesoporous TiO2 layer to harvest incident sunlight, through the injection of photo-excited electrons into the conduction band of the n-type semiconductor, i.e. TiO2. The photo conversion efficiency of the QDSSCs strongly depends on the four factors like excited state (LUMO) and ground state (HOMO) of the sensitizer GQDs, Fermi level of Pt anode and red-ox potential of the electrolyte (I3/I-) in the electrochemical reaction. The mechanism can be written as:
(1) GQDs + hυ → GQDs*
(2) GQDs* + Pt (CE) → GQDs* + e- CB (Pt)
(3) GQDs + +3I- → GQDs + I3-
(4) I3- +2e- (catalysts) → 3I- (electrolyte regeneration)

The energy band alignments for G-GQDs and R-GQDs sensitized solar cell devices are shown in Fig. 5b. It can be clearly observed from the band alignment that the migration of photo-generated electrons from R-GQDs to TiO2 is more favorable than that from G-GQDs. This, when coupled with higher photon absorption capability in case of R-GQDs, results in enhanced generation of photo-generated charge carriers and more efficient collection of the carriers at the electrodes. Both of these may be beneficial for the superior photo-conversion efficiency of R-GQDs sensitized device. The current–voltage (I-V) characteristics of the fabricated devices are presented in the Fig. 5c. Corresponding I-V curve of both G-GQDs and R-GQDs based device clearly suggest there the photovoltaic performances reflected by the short circuit current density (Jsc), open circuit voltage (Voc), fill factor (FF) and efficiency (
η
) are much superior for R-GQD sensitized device. Here we have used a solar simulator working at Solar Air Mass (AM) 1.5 G conditions, which is equivalent to 100 mW/cm2 of irradiance. The fill-factor (FF) and efficiency (
η
) of the device has been calculated using the following:
(5)
FF =
V
max
×
J
max
V
OC
×
J
SC
(6)
η =
P
max
P
in
=
V
max
×
J
max
P
in
=
V
oc
×
J
SC
× F F
P
in
where Pmax, Pin, Vmax and Jmax are maximum output power density, power density of the incident light, maximum voltage and current density in the J-V graph, respectively.

Under the illumination the R-GQDs device shows a Voc value of ∼ 0.6 V, as compared to ∼ 0.4 V for G-GQDs based device. About 250% enhancement in short circuit current density is observed for R-GQDs (14.5 mA/cm2) over the G-GQDs (5.8 mA/cm2) based device. A significant increase of both Jsc & Voc is attributed to the enhanced photo-generated charge carrier concentration, higher carrier lifetime and higher conductivity of R-GQDs. As we have discussed earlier, that R-GQDs have extended conjugations compared to G-GQDs, which assist the transport of charge carrier efficiently through the conductive sp2 carbon network. Also, from the XPS study we have observed that G-GQDs have larger content of surface functional groups specially –COOH along the basal plane as well as edge, both of which deteriorate the charge transport process due to the presence of non-conductive saturated carbon frameworks. Additionally, the concentration of nitrogen doping is quite higher in R-GQDs than G-GQDs, as evident from the XPS spectra, leading to the improvement of the current density in the device. The fill factor (FF) of the R-GQDs based device (∼61%), which determines the maximum power output of a solar cell device, is almost double of that of the G-GQDs one. The spectral response showing the external quantum efficiency (EQE) of G-GQDs and R-GQDs sensitized solar cell devices are presented in Fig. 5d. Both the spectra exhibit two distinct peaks at ∼ 460 nm and ∼ 590 nm. These peak positions are quite similar to that observed in absorption spectra. As expected, the EQE value for R-GQDs device is significantly higher due to higher optical absorption coefficient and better separation and collection of photo-generated carriers in R-GQDs. The comparative photovoltaic parameters of N-doped GQDs sensitized solar cells are presented in Table 1 , where a drastic enhancement of photo-conversion efficiency is observed for R-GQDs (5.3%) as compared to G-GQDs (0.7%).

There are several factors which affect the device fill factor (FF) like series resistance, shunt resistance and charge carrier recombination due to defects in the materials. To evaluate the resistance parameters, we have performed electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of both the solar cell devices made from G-GQDs and R-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 6 . The Nyquist curves have been fitted using the equivalent circuit shown in Fig. 6a. C1 and R1 are respectively the junction capacitance and resistance for the counter electrode/electrolyte interface. Similarly, C2 and R2 are the parameters for the interface between GQDs embedded TiO2 layer and the electrolyte, while C3 and R3 correspond to the same for the diffusion of the electrolyte. Table 2 presents the extracted parameters for both the devices from Nyquist plots under dark and illumination

From the Table 2, we observe that the series resistance Rs of R-GQDs based solar cell is lower for both dark (40.52 Ω) and illuminated (25.00 Ω) conditions as compared to that of G-GQDs one. Here junction capacitance (C1) and resistance (R1) are almost comparable for both the devices. On the other hand, R2 and C2 values of two GQDs sensitized devices are found to be quite different and under illuminated condition the C2 capacitance value of R-GQDs device decreases more drastically to 3.03μF from 20.72 μF under dark, as compared to G-GQDs. Decreased capacitance implies reduced charge trapping and better transport of carriers, which improves the performance of the device (Omar et al., 2020). Similarly, the R2 value is improved in R-GQDs under both dark and illumination revealing lower hindrance to electrical charge transport. Lower junction resistance (R2) and capacitance (C2) for R-GQDs as compared to G-GQDs based devise clearly suggest better interfaces with TiO2, which affects the overall power conversion efficiency.

Finally, in Table 3 , we have compared the performance of our optimized device made from R-GQDs with existing reports in the literature. It is quite clear that our current device performance is superior to the reported results in the literature, which suggests that solution processed N-doped R-GQDs can be used as a low cost, environment friendly and stable sensitizer for future QDSSC devices.

It is known that the stability issues are still quite challenging for DSSCs and QDSSCs. We have checked the stability of our best performing device (R-GQDs) in terms of the photoconversion efficiency (PCE), as shown in the Fig. S1 in the electronic supplementary information (ESI). We found the PCE of the R-GQD device during first measurement as ∼ 5.3%, which gets reduced to ∼ 4.4% after a period of 30 days. It may be noted that the stability of the R-GQD QDSSCs is significantly better than the traditional dyes used in DSSCs, which degrade quickly under the exposure of light. The reduction of stability in our device is attributed to the volatility and leakage of the electrolytes that can be prevented by standard encapsulation and packaging techniques, which is beyond the scope of the present work.

We have synthesized nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with tunable emission and electrical properties by changing the reaction solvent. While switching from protic H2O solvent to aprotic DMF solvent, the emission wavelength of N-GQDs shifts from green to red. As-synthesized N-GQDs have been devised as an absorbing layer in quantum dots sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs). The structural, chemical, compositional and photo-physical properties of GQDs synthesized in different solvents have been studied in detail. The X-ray photoelectron and ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra clearly reveal that the N-doped GQDs with green emission (G-GQDs) have relatively shorter effective conjugation length and lesser pyrrolic nitrogen groups, because of H2O being a polar protic solvent, which donates proton to the reaction medium to suppress the elimination of water molecules. On the other hand, the polar aprotic solvent (DMF) cannot giveaway hydrogen, hence the water elimination is elevated and more nitrogen enter the carbon skeleton, in case of red emitting R-GQDs. With the increase of conjugation length and total amount of nitrogen present, the emission profile of N-GQDS is red-shifted from green to red, and also leads to the increase in carrier density and improvement of charge transport. QDSSCs fabricated from R-GQDs show much superior performance (∼5.3%) to the G-GQDs counterpart (∼0.7%) attributed to higher Jsc, Voc and fill factor. Our best R-GQDs device outperforms all the similar devices reported previously using graphene quantum dots as sensitizer material. Our results demonstrate the potential of solution processed, stable and environment friendly N-doped GQDs as efficient sensitizers to replace toxic and readily degradable dye-sensitized solar cells for low cost photovoltaic energy harvesting.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Tamal Dey acknowledges Department of Science and Technology (DST), Govt. of India for financial support through INSPIRE Research Fellowship (IF-160592). This work was partially funded by the NNETRA project supported by MEITY and DST, Govt. of India.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.solener.2022.02.048.
